details.text: Derivative: لِ – One understanding for the لِ here is that it connects to Surah Feel with the meaning that we secured you and Makkah from the people of the elephant لِ “for the reason” of securing you and your trade. Another understanding is that لِ can be used to express amazement with the meaning of how amazing is the إِيلَاف (Eelaaf) of Quraish i.e., be amazed at the favours that Allah gave to Quraish and allowed them securely to travel for trade in the winter and summer. لِ can also be understood to mean “For the reason of” i.e., for Allah making the إِيلَاف (Eelaaf) of Quraish, فَلْيَعْبُدُوا رَبَّ هَٰذَا الْبَيْتِ “Then worship the Rabb of this house”.

Derivative: إِيلَافِ comes in origin from the trilateral verb أَلِفَ - أُلْفَة (Ulfah) which means to be affectionate, at ease and harmonious with others, and to be fond of someone or something. أَلَّفَ (Allafa) in the فَعَّلَ (Fa’a’ala) pattern form and its Masdar تَعْلِيف means to cause love and affection to exist amongst people. Here إِيلَافِ is used which comes from the pattern آَلَفَ – يُؤْلِفُ – إِيلَاف (Eelaaf) also refers to bringing affection and love amongst people and softening their hearts but differs from تَعْلِيف (Ta’leef) in that إِيلَاف means to bring the love and affection immediately whilst تَعْلِيف implies that it happens over time. It can refer to the covenants that were made amongst the Quraish and brought them together. إِيلَاف (Eelaaf) can also refer to that which has been made customary and regular which can be in reference to the trade that Quraish can securely undertake which is a favour from Allah in that He made this happen. إِيلَاف (Eelaaf) in general can refer to Allah’s bring the people together into a union.

Derivative: قُرَيْشٍ – One understanding is that قُرَيْشٍ comes from تَقَرُّش (Taqarrush) which means to gather people from various distances. قُرَيْشٍ (Quraish) therefore implies a gathering of dispersed people. Another interpretation is that it comes from قِرْش (Qirsh) which refers to a great sea creature (a shark) that can destroy or damage ships. When asked about the meaning of Quraish Ibn ‘Abbas (ra) said: “It is named by the beast in the ocean that eats but is not eaten. And it overpowers but is not overpowered”. It is in the تَصْغير form of the noun which follows the فُعَيل (Fu’ail) pattern and implies a smaller version of the original, so for example طِفل (Tifl) means child whilst طُفَيل (Tufail) means a small or young child. As such a little قِرْش (Qirsh) is called قُرَيْشٍ (Quraish). The تَصْغير form can be used as play of words and with sarcasm in Arabic to mean its opposite i.e., calling something small to actually mean how big or significant it is in reference to the big beast of Quraish that lives among the Arabs. The last explanation for the meaning of قُرَيْشٍ which is also from the root of قَرْش is that it means to earn, and they were called Quraish because they used to earn and make their living from their trading in the land.

Derivative: رِحْلَةَ comes from رَحْل which refers to baggage that is taken on a journey and placed upon the riding animal. رِحْلَةَ (Rihlah) means a journey in which you carry baggage and the Arabs used to carry all of their trade merchandise in baggage on their camels and riding animals. Here it is in reference to the regular trade that the Quraish undertook to Yemen in the winter and Syria in the summer. It has been argued that رِحْلَةَ (Rihlata) is in the نَصْب (Nasb – accusative) case is because it is acting as a ظَرْف (Zharf) of place here as it is alluding to a location.

Derivative: إِيلَافِ – The mentioning of إِيلَافِ (Eelaf) again in this verse which refers to the favours of Allah can be for the purpose of reemphasis which could be to reemphasise the favour of Allah or to reemphasis the amazement attached to the favour. It can also be because this mention of إِيلَافِ (Eelaaf) is specific (خاصّ – Khaass) whilst the first was general (عامّ – ‘Aamm) in the case where this mention relates to the trade caravans of the winter and summer whilst the former mention refers to all the favours in general.

- “The journeying (رِحْلَةَ) of the winter (الشِّتَاءِ) and the summer (وَالصَّيْفِ)”. It can be observed that the winter (الشِّتَاءِ) was mentioned first and the summer (الصَّيْفِ) was mentioned second which could add emphasis to the winter as it is harder to be prosperous in the winter than the summer in trade and yet Allah even allowed them to prosper through trade during the winter. The fact that رِحْلَةَ (Rihlah - Journey) was only mentioned once for both the winter and summer although they were two different trade journeys implies that the trade of Quraish was continuous throughout the year.

Derivative: فَ – The فَ (Faa’) here means “So” or “As such” i.e., as a consequence of what was mentioned previously of Allah’s favours to you, they should worship the Rabb of this house. The فَ (Faa’) has also been linked in some interpretations to the favours mentioned in Surah Al-Fil in relation to destroying the army of Abrahah in addition to the favours mentioned in this Surah.

الَّذِي أَطْعَمَهُم مِّن جُوعٍ وَآمَنَهُم مِّنْ خَوْفٍ

- The جُوعٍ (hunger) and خَوْفٍ (fear) are in the نَكِرَة (Nakirah - indefinite) form and implies an extreme hunger and extreme fear i.e., Allah provided them with food, security and safety in circumstances which in origin were so extreme that maintaining sustenance and security would usually be near impossible.
- The مَنْ (Min) in the statements مِّن جُوعٍ and مِّنْ خَوْفٍ can imply تَعْليل (Ta’eel - reasoning) i.e., with the meaning of “in spite of the hunger” and “in spite of the fear” in reference to their natural surroundings and circumstances that should dictate hunger and fear. The مَنْ (Min) can also imply تَبْعِيد (Tab’eed – distancing) i.e., with the meaning that we fed you keeping you far removed from being in a state of hunger and we have provided you with security far away from being in a state of fear.
- The order of mentioning hunger before fear is in line with the context of the verses which discuss trade and acquiring provision. The order of the statements also conforms with the order of winter and summer mentioned in the second verse, in the case where hunger is associated with the winter when food is scarcer and fear is associated with the summer when raids are more common.
